evidence: The paper uses random forest with "importance set to 'impurity,' which measures variance of the response" to screen 1500+ pollution variables. It identifies diisocyanates as "the top environmental exposure identified by univariate Poisson regression, by lasso selection across multiple years, and by random forest" - a ranking-based conclusion. However, three lines of independent corroboration mitigate impurity bias: (1) lasso and Poisson regression independently confirm diisocyanates as most predictive, (2) "partial dependence plots demonstrate a monotonic increase," and (3) clinical validation where "areas with above-average levels of TDI had a nonsignificant increase in baseline severity but were more responsive to R. mucosa treatment."
